methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

transformations: Raw ETH mapped to sentinel ETH address in balance mapping
data_flows.trust_changes: User funds become protocol-controlled, tracked via totalSupply
exposure_risks: lockETHFor allows depositing on behalf of anyone, enabling griefing with dust
manipulation_risks: Front-running convertAllETH by sending extra ETH to inflate totalLpETH/totalSupply ratio

data_flows.source: User ERC20 WETH via lock / lockFor
transformations: WETH token → native ETH → tracked as ETH sentinel address in balances
data_flows.trust_changes: ERC20 approval consumed, WETH atomically unwrapped
exposure_risks: Fee-on-transfer WETH variant (unlikely for canonical WETH) would cause accounting mismatch
manipulation_risks: If WETH.withdraw fails silently, ETH balance wouldn't increase but totalSupply would (not realistic for canonical WETH)

data_flows.source: User ERC20 LRT tokens via lock / lockFor
sinks: balances mapping under token key (NOT totalSupply)
data_flows.trust_changes: ERC20 approval consumed, tokens held by contract
exposure_risks: Fee-on-transfer tokens cause balances[user][token] > actual contract holding
manipulation_risks: Depositing fee-on-transfer tokens inflates user balance relative to actual tokens held, potentially causing later users' claims to fail due to insufficient contract balance

transformations: All contract ETH → lpETH via deposit
totalLpETH captures full lpETH balance including any pre-existing lpETH
data_flows.trust_changes: ETH transfers to externally-set lpETH contract - trust fully dependent on owner-set address
exposure_risks: totalLpETH includes any pre-existing lpETH in contract (donation inflation)
manipulation_risks: Owner can set malicious lpETH address that steals deposited ETH
Anyone can inflate totalLpETH by sending lpETH tokens to contract before conversion
Unsolicited ETH (via receive() or selfdestruct) inflates totalBalance

data_flows.trust_changes: User's locked balance converted to proportional lpETH claim
exposure_risks: If totalSupply was inflated (e.g., fee-on-transfer WETH), individual claims would be lower than expected
manipulation_risks: If totalLpETH is inflated relative to totalSupply, early claimers get more; if deflated, everyone gets less

data_flows.source: User calls claim(LRT_token, percentage, exchange, data) after startClaimDate
sinks: Tokens sent to exchange proxy via swap
data_flows.trust_changes: User tokens transferred to 0x exchange proxy; ETH received; then ETH sent to lpETH contract
exposure_risks: address(this).balance captures ALL ETH in contract, not just swap output
Any ETH present from other sources (direct sends, selfdestruct) is stolen by claimer
manipulation_risks: Zero-amount claim exploit: if percentage*userStake < 100, userClaim rounds to 0, user spends no tokens but captures all contract ETH
Repeated calls possible since balance isn't reduced when userClaim=0
Post-conversion ETH accumulation (via receive()) can be drained by any LRT holder

path: Checks selector, inputToken, outputToken, inputTokenAmount, recipient
transformations: Assembly-based decoding of UniswapV3 and TransformERC20 calldata
sinks: exchangeProxy receives calldata and executes swap
data_flows.trust_changes: User-provided data validated on-chain then sent to trusted exchange proxy
validation_points: Selector must match UNI_SELECTOR or TRANSFORM_SELECTOR
recipient must be address(this) or address(0)
outputToken must be WETH (Uni) or ETH sentinel (Transform)
exposure_risks: UniswapV3 _decodeUniswapV3Data assumes bytes param offset is exactly 128 (standard ABI encoding)
Non-standard ABI encoding can place real encodedPath data at different offset while fake data passes validation
manipulation_risks: Attacker crafts non-standard ABI layout where validator reads fake tokens but exchange proxy processes real (different) path
TransformERC20 recipient is never validated - relies on implicit msg.sender behavior
TransformERC20 transformations array is not validated and could contain malicious sub-operations

data_flows.source: User calls withdraw(token) during grace period or emergency
path: Check emergency mode or time window
If ETH: decrease totalSupply, send ETH
sinks: ETH or tokens sent to msg.sender
validation_points: Time window checks (loopActivation < now < startClaimDate) unless emergency
ETH withdrawal blocked after startClaimDate even in emergency mode
exposure_risks: ETH transfer via low-level call - reentrancy possible but balance is zeroed first
manipulation_risks: Emergency mode does NOT help ETH depositors after convertAllETH - UseClaimInstead check blocks ETH withdrawals post-claim-date regardless of emergency mode

core_rules: Users lock ETH/WETH/LRT tokens before loopActivation deadline for points
Owner sets loop addresses which resets loopActivation to now, starts TIMELOCK grace period
After TIMELOCK, owner converts all ETH to lpETH via convertAllETH
ETH depositors claim proportional lpETH (totalLpETH * userStake / totalSupply)
LRT depositors swap tokens to ETH via 0x then mint lpETH at 1:1
LRT balances are NOT part of totalSupply - they are tracked independently
120-day fallback: if owner never calls setLoopAddresses, after 120 days users can withdraw
economic_incentives: Points earned for locking (tracked off-chain via referral events)
Grace period withdrawals forfeit accumulated points
No on-chain penalty for early withdrawal other than point forfeiture
governance_mechanisms: Single owner controls all admin functions (no timelock on admin actions)
Owner can set lpETH and vault addresses (one-time before activation)
Owner can allow new tokens at any time
Owner can toggle emergency mode at any time
Owner can be transferred without timelock
No multisig or governance voting required

edge_cases.scenario: LRT claim with percentage rounding to zero (e.g., userStake=1, _percentage=99 → userClaim = 0)
edge_cases.expected_behavior: No tokens swapped, no lpETH received, balance unchanged
edge_cases.actual_behavior: No tokens swapped, but claimedAmount = address(this).balance captures ALL ETH in contract, minting free lpETH. Balance remains unchanged allowing repeated exploitation.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Any LRT holder with even 1 wei of balance can repeatedly drain all ETH accumulated in contract post-conversion. Attack: (1) Wait for any ETH to arrive via receive(), (2) Call claim with percentage that rounds userClaim to 0, (3) Receive all ETH as lpETH, (4) Repeat since balance isn't reduced.

edge_cases.scenario: ETH in contract after convertAllETH (from direct sends or selfdestruct)
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Orphaned ETH should not be claimable by LRT users
edge_cases.actual_behavior: _claim for non-ETH tokens uses address(this).balance (not delta) for lpETH deposit, capturing all ETH including residual
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: First LRT claimer after ETH accumulates captures all residual ETH. Combined with zero-amount claim, any LRT holder can drain repeatedly without spending tokens.

edge_cases.scenario: Non-standard ABI encoding in UniswapV3 swap data
edge_cases.expected_behavior: _validateData should correctly verify the actual parameters that will be executed
edge_cases.actual_behavior: _decodeUniswapV3Data reads encodedPath length and tokens from hardcoded offsets assuming bytes param offset is 128. If the actual bytes offset is different, validator reads fake data while exchange proxy processes real different data.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker could craft calldata where validated inputToken/outputToken differ from actually-executed swap path. Practical exploitation limited by: (1) approval is only for _sellToken, (2) sellAmount is at fixed offset and correctly validated, (3) recipient is at fixed offset. Main risk is intermediate path manipulation or if exchange proxy handles edge cases differently.

edge_cases.scenario: Emergency mode activated after convertAllETH - ETH depositors
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Emergency mode should allow all users to recover funds
edge_cases.actual_behavior: withdraw(ETH) has inner check 'if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead()' that is NOT gated by emergency mode. After convertAllETH sets startClaimDate, ETH depositors cannot withdraw even in emergency - they can only claim, requiring functional lpETH contract.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If lpETH contract becomes non-functional or malicious after conversion, ETH depositors' funds are permanently locked. Emergency mode provides no escape hatch for ETH depositors post-conversion.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Balance should reflect actual received amount
edge_cases.actual_behavior: balances[receiver][token] += _amount (requested amount), but actual received is _amount - fee. Contract holds fewer tokens than total user balances indicate.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Last withdrawer/claimer for that token will find insufficient contract balance. Early claimers drain the actual tokens; late claimers' swaps or withdrawals revert.

edge_cases.scenario: totalSupply is zero when ETH claim is attempted
edge_cases.actual_behavior: userStake.mulDiv(totalLpETH, totalSupply) would divide by zero. However, if totalSupply is 0, no user can have a non-zero ETH balance, so userStake check catches this first.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: None - safely handled by the userStake == 0 check preceding the division.

edge_cases.actual_behavior: No validation. lpETH and lpETHVault set to address(0). convertAllETH would send ETH to address(0) via lpETH.deposit which would revert.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Owner misconfiguration could brick the contract. Funds recoverable only via emergency mode withdrawal (but ETH depositors still subject to UseClaimInstead limitation).

edge_cases.scenario: Multiple partial LRT claims with different exchange rates
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Each partial claim should swap at current market rate with user-specified slippage
edge_cases.actual_behavior: No contract-enforced slippage protection. _claim relies entirely on the minBuyAmount in 0x calldata. MEV attackers can sandwich each swap.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Sandwich attacks on LRT→ETH swaps. Users can mitigate via 0x calldata slippage settings but contract provides no fallback protection.

edge_cases.scenario: claim() called with _percentage = 101 for non-ETH token
edge_cases.actual_behavior: userClaim = userStake * 101 / 100 > userStake. Then balances[msg.sender][_token] = userStake - userClaim underflows and reverts (Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic).
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: None - arithmetic overflow protection prevents over-claiming. But error message is generic, not descriptive.

edge_cases.actual_behavior: After first call, loopActivation = block.timestamp. Modifier onlyBeforeDate(loopActivation) requires block.timestamp < loopActivation, which fails in same or later block. Effectively one-time.

User balance is zeroed in _claim before external calls. lpETH.approve and lpETHVault.stake are called after. Re-entry via vault would find zero balance. However, if vault calls back into claim with a different token, the user could claim that token too in the same tx. This is expected behavior if they have multiple deposits.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Limited - balance is zeroed before external calls for the specific token being claimed.

assumption_violations: CRITICAL: _claim assumes address(this).balance == 0 before swap for non-ETH tokens ('At this point there should not be any ETH'). Violated by: receive() accepting ETH, selfdestruct-forced ETH, or any post-conversion ETH deposit.
UniswapV3 calldata decoding assumes standard ABI encoding with bytes offset = 128. Non-standard but valid ABI encoding can place data at different offsets.

Assumes lpETH.deposit is 1:1 ETH→lpETH conversion. If not, totalLpETH reflects actual received amount via balanceOf, so proportional claims adjust. But contract comments and events suggest 1:1 expectation.
Assumes all allowed tokens are non-rebasing and non-fee-on-transfer. Comment says 'ONLY add wrapped LRT tokens. Contract not compatible with rebase tokens.' If violated, accounting breaks.
Assumes exchange proxy (0x) is trusted and cannot be upgraded to steal approved tokens. If 0x proxy is compromised, all approved LRT tokens can be drained.
Assumes owner is trusted and won't set malicious lpETH/vault addresses. No governance, timelock, or multisig protection.
game_theory_risks: Post-conversion ETH theft: Any ETH sent to contract after convertAllETH can be stolen by first LRT claimer. Combined with zero-amount claim exploit, this is repeatable without cost.
MEV sandwich attacks on LRT→ETH swaps: No contract-level slippage protection means MEV bots can extract value from every LRT claim swap.
Front-running convertAllETH: Sending ETH to contract before conversion to inflate totalLpETH ratio benefits all ETH depositors proportionally. Not profitable for attacker unless sole depositor.
Owner can grief users by never calling setLoopAddresses (funds locked until 120-day fallback) or by setting malicious lpETH address.

Race between grace period withdrawals and convertAllETH: Users who want to withdraw must do so before owner calls convertAllETH. Owner can reduce this window by calling convertAllETH as soon as TIMELOCK expires.
integration_risks: 0x Exchange Proxy dependency: Contract forwards user-crafted calldata to 0x proxy. If 0x proxy has vulnerabilities or is upgraded maliciously, funds at risk.
lpETH contract dependency: All ETH is deposited into owner-set lpETH address. Malicious or buggy lpETH contract can steal all deposited ETH.
lpETHVault dependency: claimAndStake approves and stakes lpETH into vault. Malicious vault can steal approved lpETH.
WETH dependency: WETH.withdraw() is called during deposit. Non-standard WETH could fail or behave unexpectedly.
TransformERC20 transformations array is fully user-controlled and not validated. Complex transformation chains could interact with contract in unexpected ways.
calculation_errors: CRITICAL: In _claim for non-ETH tokens, claimedAmount = address(this).balance uses total balance instead of swap output delta. Should use boughtETHAmount pattern from _fillQuote.

Percentage rounding: userClaim = userStake * _percentage / 100 rounds down. For small userStake values, userClaim can be 0 even with non-zero percentage, allowing zero-cost claims that capture all contract ETH.
convertAllETH: totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(address(this)) includes any pre-existing lpETH tokens, not just those received from the deposit. Should use balance delta.
TIMELOCK check uses <= instead of <: 'block.timestamp - loopActivation <= TIMELOCK' means actual timelock is TIMELOCK + 1 seconds. Minor but imprecise.

token_semantics.notes: Sentinel address for native ETH tracking. Used as key in balances mapping. ETH deposits tracked via totalSupply.
Immutable address set at construction. Deposits auto-unwrap to ETH via WETH.withdraw(). isTokenAllowed[WETH] = true. Cannot be recovered via recoverERC20.
Set by owner via setLoopAddresses. Used with SafeERC20. deposit() expected to mint 1:1 with ETH. Cannot be recovered via recoverERC20.

Owner-whitelisted tokens. Contract assumes non-rebasing, non-fee-on-transfer per code comments. Fee-on-transfer tokens would break accounting. Used with SafeERC20 for transfers but raw approve() in _fillQuote.

invariants.name: eth_supply_conservation
invariants.notes: totalSupply should equal sum of all ETH balances. Maintained by lock (+) and withdraw (-) operations. Violated if ETH is sent directly to contract.

invariants.name: lrt_balance_conservation
invariants.notes: Contract token balance should >= sum of user balances for each LRT. VIOLATED by fee-on-transfer tokens where balanceOf < sum(balances).

invariants.name: lpeth_distribution_conservation
invariants.notes: Total claimed lpETH from ETH deposits should equal totalLpETH. Each claim: userStake * totalLpETH / totalSupply. Sum should converge to totalLpETH as all users claim.

invariants.name: post_conversion_eth_zero
invariants.notes: ASSUMED invariant: after convertAllETH, address(this).balance should be 0. VIOLATED by receive() accepting ETH, selfdestruct, or any external ETH transfer. Violation enables free lpETH minting by LRT claimers.

invariants.name: phase_monotonicity
invariants.notes: Contract phases progress: deposit → grace period → claim. loopActivation moves forward when setLoopAddresses called. startClaimDate set once by convertAllETH. Emergency mode can be toggled at any time, breaking normal phase progression.
